topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the sterile finality of a death sentence. My stomach clenched, a tight, cold knot forming just beneath my ribs. I clicked it open, my fingers trembling slightly over the screen, and read the headline. My name wasn't there. Instead, the byline belonged to a stranger, someone whose name I vaguely recognized from a previous, unrelated publication. A slow burn started in my chest, a dull, heavy ache that radiated outwards. I stared at the screen, the words blurring momentarily. I wanted to scream, but the sound caught in my throat, leaving only a dry rasp. I stood up abruptly, pacing the length of my small apartment, running a hand repeatedly through my already disheveled hair. Every surface seemed too bright, too sharp. The air felt thick, suffocating.

[story 2]

I found the notification tucked away in a seldom-checked folder. The article, my meticulously crafted piece on urban ecology, was out there, under a different moniker. A wave of nausea washed over me, making my vision swim. I sank onto the edge of my desk, the wood cool and unforgiving beneath my palms. My breath hitched, shallow and quick. I looked at the screen again, focusing on the unfamiliar signature. A strange, hollow feeling settled deep in my core, a profound sense of being overlooked, of my effort being erased. I pressed my lips together until they were white, fighting the sudden, intense urge to crumple the paper into a tight ball. I stood up, moving to the window, watching the city lights flicker—each one a distant, uncaring spark.

[story 3]

When I saw the publication date, the realization hit with the force of a physical blow. It wasn't me. The text was undeniably mine, the research, the voice, but the attribution was entirely wrong. A strange, distant tremor ran through my hands; I gripped the edge of the table until my knuckles turned white. My jaw tightened, muscles bunching beneath my skin. I leaned back, the chair groaning in protest, and closed my eyes, trying to steady the rapid drumming in my chest. When I opened them, the room seemed suddenly too loud, the silence amplifying the frantic thumping within me. I began to methodically gather my notes, folding them with an almost violent precision, as if trying to physically contain the unexpected upheaval inside.
